text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 2 August 2022 

Dr Patel  
St George's Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-11-1988, Hospital No: H527391 NHS No. 9081764523  
52 Willowbank Close, London, SW12 0AA  

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder (aetiology not established). 

Thank you for asking me to review Michael Turner in clinic today. He attended with his partner and described a period earlier this year when several doses of his regular anti-seizure medication were missed due to shift work changes and variable sleep. Since our last contact, he has implemented smartphone alarm schedules (morning and evening) and set up NFC tags at his bedside and kitchen to confirm dose administration; the phone records a timestamp when the tag is tapped. He feels this has improved consistency, and his partner has visibility of adherence via the shared app. There have been no adverse effects reported from medication and no new neurological symptoms. 

Seizure record (patient-reported and cross-checked with his app timestamps): Seizure: 2022: Jan x1, Feb x0, Mar x1, Apr x2, May x1, Jun x1, Jul x1. He notes typical events are brief with preserved awareness at onset, a déjà vu sensation, metallic taste, and occasional right-hand fumbling; duration under two minutes, followed by mild fatigue. No injuries, no emergency attendances. There is no clear photic sensitivity. Sleep disruption and missed doses were the most consistent antecedents before the alarm/NFC system was introduced. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. Adherence since mid-April has been high per app logs (missed or delayed doses now rare); he has enabled a 30-minute escalation alarm to his partner. Concomitants: vitamin D supplement only. He denies mood change beyond transient irritability in the first week of therapy, now resolved. 

Examination today was non-focal. Vitals within normal limits. No tremor, coordination intact, gait stable. 

Investigations: Prior MRI brain (2021) reported as normal. Prior routine EEG (2021) non-specific without definite epileptiform discharges. We discussed the option of sleep-deprived EEG if breakthrough events increase, though at present he prefers to continue observation. 

Management and plan: 
- Continue current medication dose; no changes made today. 
- Persist with smartphone alarms and NFC tap confirmation; his app analytics suggest adherence >95% since implementation. 
- Safety: no driving until legally eligible; he confirms he is currently not driving. Workplace has been informed; he avoids lone work at heights. 
- If seizure clusters recur or events generalise, he will contact us via the advice line; we would then consider ambulatory EEG and serum level check. 
- Follow-up in 4–6 months, sooner if required. 

Additional notes: He reports that the NFC tags occasionally fail to register if the phone case is misaligned; he has repositioned the tags and set a secondary alarm as backup. He and his partner find the shared log reassuring. There have been no emergency rescue medications used. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides a month-by-month seizure count for 2022 up to July: Jan 1, Feb 0, Mar 1, Apr 2, May 1, Jun 1, Jul 1. Total from Jan–Jul = 1+0+1+2+1+1+1 = 7 seizures over 7 months. No explicit more recent frequency beyond July is given, and there is no evidence of 6 months seizure freedom. Therefore, normalize to 7 per 7 month.
